text:
HEAD AND NECK MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
SURVEILLANCE FOLLOW-UP NOTE — 24-MONTH POST-TREATMENT VISIT

ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
CC: [Fellow/Resident]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Routine 2-year surveillance visit. HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, base of tongue, previously treated with definitive concurrent chemoradiation.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Chen is a 54-year-old never-smoking East Asian woman with a history of Stage III (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition, HPV-positive staging system) squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, p16 strongly positive by IHC and HPV-16 confirmed by ISH, who presents today for her 24-month post-treatment surveillance evaluation. She completed definitive IMRT (70 Gy in 35 fractions to the primary and gross nodal disease, with 63 Gy and 56 Gy to high-risk and low-risk elective volumes, respectively) with concurrent cisplatin 100 mg/m2 every 3 weeks — she received 2 of 3 planned cycles due to cycle-3 hold for nephrotoxicity (creatinine peak 1.32 mg/dL, fully recovered). Treatment course was notable for grade 3 mucositis requiring PEG tube placement for nutritional support, as well as grade 2 radiation dermatitis, grade 1 peripheral neuropathy, and grade 1 ototoxicity at high frequencies. She achieved a complete metabolic response on post-treatment PET-CT at 12 weeks. PEG tube was decannulated approximately 4 months post-treatment. She has remained without evidence of disease through all subsequent surveillance visits at 6, 9, 12, and 18 months.

Today she reports feeling well overall. She denies any new throat discomfort, odynophagia, dysphagia, or foreign body sensation. She has no hoarseness, no otalgia, no new neck swelling, and no constitutional symptoms including fevers, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss. She has returned to full-time work as a high school music teacher and describes her quality of life as excellent. She continues levothyroxine for radiation-induced hypothyroidism, which is well-controlled on her current dose.

Her primary ongoing complaint remains mild xerostomia, which she characterizes as stable and substantially improved compared to the acute treatment period. She manages it with frequent water intake, over-the-counter saliva substitutes (Biotene spray), and sugarless gum. She reports no dental caries or new dental issues. Swallowing is functionally normal; she eats a general diet without restriction, though she continues to keep beverages nearby when eating dry foods such as bread or crackers. She denies any coughing or choking with meals. She reports no tinnitus flares or new hearing changes beyond the mild high-frequency loss documented at her 12-month audiogram. Peripheral neuropathy in the fingertips has fully resolved. She is not taking any opioid analgesics.

She is engaged in the cancer survivorship program and has become an advocate for HPV vaccination among students and parents in her school community.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive: Mild persistent xerostomia (stable, improved).
Negative: No odynophagia. No dysphagia. No hoarseness. No otalgia or new ear complaints. No hemoptysis or blood in sputum. No neck mass or swelling. No fevers. No night sweats. No weight loss. No fatigue beyond baseline. No headaches. No vision changes. No chest pain, shortness of breath, or cough. No bone pain. No neurological symptoms. No new skin changes of the neck.

---

CURRENT MEDICATIONS
- Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally daily (for radiation-induced hypothyroidism; TSH 1.9 mIU/L at 18-month visit)
- Biotene oral spray as needed for xerostomia
- Multivitamin daily

ALLERGIES: No known drug allergies.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished East Asian woman in no acute distress. Appears her stated age. Alert and oriented x3. Conversational, pleasant affect.

Vitals: Weight 59 kg (baseline pre-treatment 62 kg; net loss ~3 kg from pre-treatment weight, stable over the past several months). BP 118/72 mmHg. HR 68 bpm. RR 16. Temperature 36.8°C. SpO2 99% on room air.

ECOG Performance Status: 0.

Head and Neck:
- Face: Symmetric. No facial edema.
- Oral cavity: Mucosa appears well-healed. No ulceration, leukoplakia, or erythematous lesions. Mild mucosal dryness consistent with xerostomia. Teeth appear intact with no obvious new caries on visual inspection. Tongue mobile, no visible masses on anterior or lateral tongue.
- Oropharynx: Posterior pharyngeal wall without erythema or mass. Tonsils absent (natural atrophy, no surgical resection). Posterior pillars symmetric.
- Flexible nasopharyngoscopy performed at today's visit: The nasopharynx is clear. The left base of tongue demonstrates well-healed mucosa, smooth and without any visible lesion, ulceration, nodularity, or abnormal vascularity. The right base of tongue and valleculae are unremarkable. The supraglottis and true vocal cords are visualized — cords are symmetric, mobile bilaterally, no supraglottic or glottic lesions seen. Hypopharynx without abnormality. No pooling of secretions. Appearance consistent with complete healing post-radiation and no evidence of recurrent disease.
- Neck: Soft. No palpable cervical, submandibular, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy bilaterally. Level I through V assessed. Mild bilateral anterior cervical fibrosis consistent with prior radiation field; stable compared to prior examinations. No mass effect or skin breakdown over the radiated field.
- Thyroid: Not enlarged on palpation.
- Ears: Bilateral external auditory canals without obstruction. Tympanic membranes intact bilaterally.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II–XII grossly intact. No facial asymmetry. No tongue deviation. Shoulder shrug symmetric. Fine motor function in hands intact; no residual neuropathy detected on clinical exam.

Lymph nodes: No peripheral lymphadenopathy (axillary, inguinal).

Cardiopulmonary: Heart: regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Lungs: clear to auscultation bilaterally.

Extremities: No edema. No joint swelling.

---

LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS

Most recent labs reviewed from prior visit (18 months post-treatment):
- TSH: 1.9 mIU/L (normal on levothyroxine 75 mcg)
- Free T4: within normal limits
- Creatinine: 0.82 mg/dL (returned to near-baseline after post-cisplatin elevation; stable)
- CBC: within normal limits

Today's visit labs not yet resulted (TSH, BMP ordered; to be reviewed at next visit or as results available).

Flexible nasopharyngoscopy: as above — no evidence of recurrent or residual disease.

Imaging: CT neck with contrast ordered for today's 2-year post-treatment surveillance per our protocol. Results pending at time of this note. Will be reviewed and addended or discussed at next contact.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, left base of tongue, Stage III (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition, p16+, HPV-16+), status post definitive concurrent cisplatin-IMRT — complete response maintained, no evidence of disease at 24-month surveillance.

Ms. Chen continues to do exceptionally well at her 2-year mark. Completion of 2 years of disease-free surveillance is a highly meaningful milestone for HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC; the literature is quite reassuring that the vast majority of HPV-related oropharyngeal cancers that recur do so within the first 2 years, and those achieving complete response with maintained remission at this point carry a favorable long-term prognosis. Physical examination and flexible nasopharyngoscopy today are entirely negative for locoregional recurrence. I have counseled her on this milestone and its significance. She was appropriately reassured and expressed gratitude for the care team's efforts.

Surveillance CT of the neck with contrast has been ordered and will be reviewed. Given two years of clear imaging and clinical examination, we plan to transition surveillance to clinical examination every 6 months, with imaging guided by clinical findings rather than scheduled annually, consistent with National Comprehensive Cancer Network (NCCN) guidelines for this stage and histology in the context of complete response. She was informed of this transition. I emphasized the importance of continued surveillance and the symptoms she should watch for — new neck mass, throat pain, odynophagia, hoarseness, unilateral otalgia, hemoptysis — and that she should contact us promptly for any such concerns rather than waiting for her scheduled visit.

De-escalation discussion: The patient and I again briefly discussed the evolving landscape of de-escalated therapy for HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC. I explained that ongoing and recently completed trials (e.g., evaluating reduced radiation dose or induction chemotherapy followed by response-adapted treatment) are informing future management of newly diagnosed patients and may benefit the next generation of patients with her disease. As she has already received and completed standard therapy with an excellent outcome, these data are most relevant for newly diagnosed patients. She understands this and expressed interest in sharing this information within her advocacy work.

2. Radiation-induced hypothyroidism — well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily.
TSH 1.9 mIU/L at 18-month visit; trending appropriately. TSH rechecked today; results pending. Continue current dose pending results. Will dose-adjust as needed.

3. Xerostomia, grade 1, chronic, stable — secondary to parotid and minor salivary gland radiation.
Managed conservatively with Biotene spray, hydration, sugarless gum. No pilocarpine initiated at this time; patient tolerating current management well. Counseled on continued dental hygiene, fluoride trays if indicated, and semi-annual dental follow-up to screen for radiation-related caries. Encouraged continued vigilance.

4. High-frequency sensorineural hearing loss, grade 1, bilateral — stable, attributable to cisplatin ototoxicity.
No subjective worsening. Audiogram not repeated today; will obtain if she reports any new subjective hearing changes. No intervention indicated at this time.

5. Survivorship and psychosocial support.
Patient is thriving, engaged, and back to full-time meaningful work. She has channeled her experience into HPV vaccination advocacy at her school. She is connected to the survivorship program. No depression or anxiety screening concerns today. Continued engagement encouraged.

FOLLOW-UP: Return to head and neck oncology in 6 months for next surveillance examination. Imaging to be ordered based on clinical findings. TSH and renal function to be rechecked at that visit. Patient instructed to call with any new or concerning symptoms before scheduled visit.

Time spent with patient: approximately 45 minutes, greater than 50% counseling and coordination of care.

— Electronically signed, Head and Neck Medical Oncology
